summary.brief: Ghostanyans v. Goodwin. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: Plaintiffs Christine Ghostanyans and Tadeh Ghostanyans, individually and as parents and next friends of Landon Ghostanyans, a minor, appeal a judgment following a jury trial for defendants Pamela Goodwin, M.D., and Northshore Associates in Gynecology & Obstetrics, S.C.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: This is a medical malpractice case regarding the August 24, 2012, birth of Landon Ghostanyans to plaintiffs, delivered by physicians, including Goodwin and Loren Hutter, M.D., at a hospital of Northshore University Health System (System).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: During Landon’s birth, Goodwin diagnosed a shoulder dystocia, which occurs when a fetus’s shoulder gets blocked by or caught upon the mother’s pubic bone during birth.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Landon suffered an avulsion of his C8 nerve root, brachial plexus palsy, injury to his phrenic nerve, and diaphragmatic palsy at the time of his delivery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Gubernick opined that Goodwin deviated from the standard of care for a reasonably careful obstetrician during Landon’s delivery.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: The judgment in favor of Goodwin and Associates is affirmed.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: We find that there was evidence that, if believed by the jury, would reasonably cause it to conclude that Hutter applied excessive traction and Goodwin did not, so that Goodwin did not cause Landon’s permanent brachial plexus injury.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: During the delivery of Landon Ghostanyans on August 24, 2012, shoulder dystocia was diagnosed by Dr. Goodwin.
critical_findings: Turtle sign indicating possible shoulder dystocia

requestables.name: Obstetrician Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation with obstetrician regarding delivery complications

timeline_phases.name: Labor and Delivery Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient Christine Ghostanyans in labor at hospital

timeline_phases.name: Delivery Procedure
timeline_phases.description: Attempt to relieve shoulder dystocia during delivery

decision_points.clinical_context: During delivery with diagnosed shoulder dystocia
decision_points.prompt: What maneuver should be used to relieve shoulder dystocia?

options.description: Apply gentle traction and perform McRoberts maneuver
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to relieve shoulder dystocia without excessive force
Excessive traction can cause brachial plexus injury

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed to be necessary to relieve dystocia

expected_action_court.description: Gentle traction with appropriate maneuvers should have been used
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated excessive traction is a deviation from standard care

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Excessive traction is known to cause brachial plexus injuries
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Gentle traction with maneuvers like McRoberts should have been used
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Landon suffered a brachial plexus injury
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Potential limitations in upper limb function

key_findings: Goodwin did not apply excessive traction
Hutter's actions were considered the sole proximate cause

factual_timeline.event: Landon Ghostanyans born with shoulder dystocia
Diagnosis of shoulder dystocia during delivery

allegations.allegation: Failure to use appropriate maneuvers for shoulder dystocia
Excessive traction causing brachial plexus injury

court_findings.finding: No liability found for Dr. Goodwin
court_findings.legal_significance: Jury found another person was the sole proximate cause

court_findings.finding: Goodwin did not apply excessive traction
court_findings.legal_significance: No breach of standard of care
